title: Extraction of Meiofauna and Meioflora from Aquatic Environmental Samples
author: Daisuke Shimada

steps.title: Collect substrates (sediments, seagrasses, seaweeds, sessile animals, etc
steps.instruction: Collect substrates (sediments, seagrasses, seaweeds, sessile animals, etc.) by hand or by using sampling devices.

steps.title: Transfer 100–500 ml of substrate into...
steps.instruction: Transfer 100–500 ml of substrate into a bucket and wash the substrate well with approximately 10 times volume of tap water. If the substrate is mud, reduce the substrate volume to 50 ml or less. For fragile animals, such as turbellarians, use 3.5% MgCl2 instead of tap water and stir gently. Wait for 10 min or more and stir again gently. References: Hulings & Gray (1971); Pfannkuche & Thirl (1988); Giere (2009) Giere O. Sampling and processing meiofauna. In: Giere O. Meinbeothology: the microscopic motile fauna of aquatic sediments. Second edition. Berlin; Springer, pp. 63–86. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-540-68661-3_3 A manual for the study of meiofauna. Smithsonian Contribution of Zoology. 78: 1–83. Sample processing. In: Higgins RP, Thiel H, editors. Introduction to the study of meiofauna. Washington, D.C. & London; Smithonian Institute Press.

steps.title: Transport entire substrates to the laboratory...
steps.instruction: Transport entire substrates to the laboratory at low temperature or immediately filter and fix the living organisms in the field by following the steps below. If performing this step is difficult, substrates should be frozen or stored in the DESS solution, despite the risk of injury to organisms.

steps.title: Immediately pass the supernatants through a...
steps.instruction: Immediately pass the supernatants through a 10–100 μm (usually 32 or 63 μm) nylon mesh filter. Smaller mesh sizes can collect more organisms but are easily clogged with debris.

steps.title: Repeat steps 3 and 4 once or twice
steps.instruction: Repeat steps 3 and 4 once or twice.

steps.title: Carefully remove the nylon mesh from...
steps.instruction: Carefully remove the nylon mesh from the bottle, turn over, and wash in a Petri dish with 20 ml of filtered seawater. When handling a large substrate amounts or when the mesh appears clogged, the collected material can be transferred to a Petri dish with a dropper after each filtration.

steps.title: Transfer the whole seawater with collected...
steps.instruction: Transfer the whole seawater with collected material to a 25–50 ml sampling bottle. If necessary, divide into several bottles.

steps.title: Heat the sampling bottle in 80–90°C...
steps.instruction: Heat the sampling bottle in 80–90°C water for 2 min to kill the organisms and to inactivate the DNase. Several animals, especially nematodes, straighten when killed by heating. When looking for smaller animals, such as tardigrades, which are easier to find when they are alive, the order of heat killing, fixation, and sorting can be switched.

steps.title: Add 5 to 10 times the...
steps.instruction: Add 5 to 10 times the volume of DESS solution to the collected material and mix well. The sample can be stored in this state for a long period (more than 10 years). Reference: Yoder et al. (2006) Yoder M, Tandingan De Ley I, King IW, Mundo-Ocampo M, Mann J, Blaxter M, Poiras L, De Ley P. DESS: a versatile solution for preserving morphology and extractable DNA of nematodes. Nematology. 8: 367–376. https://doi.org/10.1163/156854106778493448

steps.title: How to make 1 liter of DESS solution Dissolve 93
steps.instruction: How to make 1 liter of DESS solution Dissolve 93.0 g of 2Na-EDTA in 400 ml of DW by stirring while gradually adding NaOH. Adjust pH to 7.5–8.0 and add DW until the total volume reaches 500 ml (= 0.5M EDTA). *Buying a pre-conditioned EDTA solution is less time-consuming (but more expensive).

steps.title: Transfer the sample to a Petri dish
steps.instruction: Transfer the sample to a Petri dish. Pick up organisms from the collected material using an Irwin loop under a stereomicroscope and store in 2.0-ml screw-cap micro tubes of DESS solution. To prevent rusting, rinse the loop with DW after each use. References: Shirayama (2004); Schram & Davison (2012) Kaisan senchu [Marine nematodes]. In: Mamiya Y, Araki M, Ishibashi N, Iwahori H, Ogura N, Kosaka H, et al., editors. Senchugaku Jikkenho [Nematological Experimentation]. Tsukuba: The Japanese Nematological Society; pp. 187–194.. Schram MD, Davison PG. Irwin loops—a history and method of constructing homemade loops. Transactions of the Kansas Academy of Science. 115: 35–40. https://doi.org/10.1660/062.115.0106

steps.title: How to make an Irwin loop...
steps.instruction: How to make an Irwin loop Cut a stainless-steel wire (0.2–0.3 mm diameter) into 10 cm and 5 cm pieces.

steps.title: Mix 500 ml of 0
steps.instruction: Mix 500 ml of 0.5 M EDTA with 200 ml (or 200 g) of DMSO and add DW until a final volume of 1,000 ml.

steps.title: Add a saturated amount of NaCl...
steps.instruction: Add a saturated amount of NaCl and stir well (precipitation of NaCl is not a problem).

steps.title: How to make a filter Remove...
steps.instruction: How to make a filter Remove the bottom from a 500 or 1,000 ml wide-mouth screw-cap plastic bottle and cut a piece of nylon mesh larger than the mouth of the bottle. How to make a filter.

steps.title: Place the mesh over the mouth...
steps.instruction: Place the mesh over the mouth of the bottle and fasten tightly with rubber bands. A completed filiter.

steps.title: Hook the pieces together and twist...
steps.instruction: Hook the pieces together and twist them using pliers, leaving small loops. How to make an Irwin loop.

steps.title: Remove the shorter piece and fix...
steps.instruction: Remove the shorter piece and fix the longer piece to a suitable handle with water-resistant adhesive.

steps.title: Bend the loop part to a manageable angle with pliers
steps.instruction: Bend the loop part to a manageable angle with pliers. A completed Irwin loop, fixed to a disposable chopstick.

steps.title: Wait 10 min for the organisms...
steps.instruction: Wait 10 min for the organisms to settle at the bottom and remove as much of the supernatant as possible with a dropper.
